ATTORNEYS AT LAW
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

GLENN R. WATSON,
ROBERT G. BEVERLY
CHARLES E. GERSHON
DOUGLAS W. ARQUE
MARK L. LAMKEN
WILLIAM F. CROUCH

STEVEN F. HEINEMANN
STEVEN L. DORSEY

TIMOTHY W. MOORE
GREGORY W. STEPANOVICH
JAMES P. BROWNE
DONALD STERN
SCOTT M. EDSON
WILLIAM S. RUDELL
DAVID L. CICHIER
VICTOR KOVICS
CAROLL W. LYNCH
DONALD M. WALSH, JR.
JOHN A. BELCHER
MATTHEW C. KAHN
GREGORY M. KUNERT
SCOTT WEIBLER
MICHAEL SCHATZ
MICHELE SEAL BAGNERIS
WILLIAM K. KRAMER

CURTIS L. COLEMAN
STEVEN H. KAUPMANN
MARGARET E. PATHE
AMANDA P. SUSSKING
WILLIAM E. MATSUOKA
PETER W. HARPER
PAMELA A. AUBERS
KEVIN B. MWAYA
KEVIN A. CASEY
MICHAEL ESTRADA
LELAND C. CRESS
WILLIAM P. SHEEHAN
CHRISTI HOGIN
STEVEN R. ORR
LINDA A. SPARKMAN
SCOTT K. SHINTAN
CRISTINA RAMIREZ-DUVONO
JACK S. SHOCKETT
SUSAN L. WILSON
DARYL T. TESHIMA
CHRISTINA R. MELTZER
KEVIN B. ABEL
R. ALAN EKE
PAUL E. WEINER
SABRA T. ASAMURA
KENNETH D. CLOKE
TAYLOR L. FITZMAURICE
DAVID M. FLEISHMAN
ERIC D. SUմիsion
STEVEN L. HOLCOMB

RICHARD RICHARDS
OF COUNSEL

THIRTY-EIGHTH FLOOR
333 SOUTH HOPE STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90071-1489
(213) 626-8484
TELEPHONE (213) 620-0078

September 25, 1992

OF COUNSEL
RICHARD J. ABEL

1090773

OUR FILE NUMBER

10190-00002


VIA FAX AND MAIL

Bianca Breeze, Esq.
Staff Attorney
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 600
Sacramento, California 95812

Re: Request for Formal Written Advice
[Government Code § 83114(b); FPPC
Regulation 18329]

Dear Ms. Breeze:

Our office represents the City of La Puente ("City") as
City Attorney. Mayor Louis R. Perez has authorized us to request
formal written advice from the Fair Political Practices
Commission ("FPPC"). The question is whether Mayor Perez has a
financial interest which prohibits him from participating in
decisions involving the proposed Downtown Specific Plan (the
"Specific Plan"). This request is based on the following facts:

The City has retained consultants who are working with
staff on developing a potential Downtown Specific Plan.1/ We
are informed that they have a preliminary working draft, that is
undergoing further refinement. The boundaries of the Specific
Plan are currently proposed to be the old downtown commercial
area of the City (the "Plan Area"). The general objective is to
upgrade the downtown area into a viable commercial center.

_______________________

1/ A Specific Plan is authorized by, and described in,
Government Code Sections 65450-65457.

RICHARDS WATSON & GERSHON

Blanca Breeze, Esq.
September 25, 1992
Page 2

The City has scheduled a joint meeting of the City
Council and Planning Commission on October 19, 1992. At that
meeting, the initial concept for the Specific Plan will be
presented to the Council, Commission, and public. The purpose of
the October 19 meeting is for staff and the consultants to obtain
guidance from the Council and Planning Commission on what
direction the Specific Plan should take. No documents will be
presented for approval at the October 19 meeting. There will be
additional meetings involving the Specific Plan before it is
presented to the Commission and ultimately to the Council for
adoption [assuming the proposed Specific Plan ever gets that
far].

A Specific Plan does not authorize a public agency to
acquire property for the purpose of implementing the Plan. The
Plan is merely a planning document which the City hopes will
guide new development. It is anticipated that the Plan will
require that new development be constructed in conformity with
the Plan, but that existing buildings can be operated and
repaired even though they are not consistent with the Plan.
Further, there is no amortization schedule that would require
demolition of any building in the Plan Area. Since the City will
not have the power to condemn property to implement the Plan,
implementation will be dependant upon existing property owners
constructing improvements in compliance with the Plan or upon new
developers acquiring property and constructing new buildings
pursuant to the Plan's objectives. It is likely, therefore, that
it will be many years, if ever, before the Plan is implemented.

We are informed that Mayor Perez' wife is an employee
of a woman's retail clothing business (the "business") located in
the Plan Area. We are further informed that the owner of the
Business also owns the building and real property on which the
Business is located, and that the building houses one other
storefront that is currently vacant.

Pursuant to Section 82030(a), his community property
interest in his wife's income is a source of income to Mayor
Perez. We are informed that she earned more than $500 from the
business within the past twelve months. Accordingly, his
community property interest in that income will exceed $250.

The initial concept of the Specific Plan calls for the
redesign of several blocks in the Plan Area to create central
courtyards and to encourage pedestrian activity. One of the
blocks proposed to be redesigned is the block on which the
Business that employs Mrs. Perez is located. Ultimate
implementation of the redesign of that block would require the
demolition of the building housing the Business. The building
owner is not obligated to demolish the building, however. The

RICHARDS WATSON & GERSHON

Blanca Breeze, Esq.
September 25, 1992
Page 3

building and Business could remain indefinitely if the property
owner does not desire to construct a new building.

Thus, if a property owner ever implements the redesign
of the block on which the Business is located, implementation of
the Specific Plan would likely cause the relocation of the
business that employs Mrs. Perez. There is therefore a remote
possibility that adoption of the Specific Plan would lead to the
demolition of the building that houses the Business.

We are troubled by the remoteness of any potential
impact on the Business. Unlike a redevelopment plan, the
Specific Plan does not grant to the City the authority to buy the
property and demolish the building where it is located. The
Specific Plan calls for the redesign of various city blocks, but
provides no implementation mechanism. As with most specific
plans, the City will have to await efforts from the private
sector in order for the Specific Plan to be implemented.
Therefore, the impact on the Business which employs Mrs. Perez is
very remote, speculative and uncertain. Adoption of the Plan
certainly will have no immediate impact on Mrs. Perez' income or
the income of the Business. The only possible impact could be on
the property on which the Business is located, but this impact
would again appear to be too speculative to be quantifiable.

In addition to the Specific Plan, we are also informed
that city staff are considering recommending adoption of a
redevelopment project. There is currently no redevelopment
project in the City. A redevelopment project, which would have
included the downtown area, was overturned by referendum on 1991.
A redevelopment project may [or may not] include the power of
eminent domain. In the event that both the Specific Plan and a
redevelopment project that includes the power of eminent domain
are adopted, and to the extent that financing is available, the
Specific Plan would be more likely to be implemented.

However, the Specific Plan can [and might] be adopted,
if at all, without adoption of a redevelopment project. There is
no requirement that a specific plan be implemented through a
redevelopment project, just as there is no requirement that a
redevelopment project also include a specific plan.

Accordingly, we request formal written advice from the
Commission on two questions:

1. Does Mayor Perez have a financial interest which
prohibits him from participating in decisions involving
the proposed Specific Plan?

RICHARDS WATSON & GERSHON

Blanca Breeze, Esq.
September 25, 1992
Page 4


     2.   Does the answer to the first question depend on whether
a redevelopment project, covering the same area as the
Specific Plan, also is adopted?

     We would appreciate your earliest response to this
matter. As mentioned above, the first meeting involving the
Specific Plan is scheduled for October 19, and the Mayor would
like to participate in this important matter if there is no
prohibited conflict of interest.


                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Estrada

cc:  Mayor Perez [15900 East Main Street,
     La Puente, California 91744]
     Robert G. Gutierrez, City Manager
     Carol W. Lynch, City Attorney

ME:me
1090773

sections.question: 1. Does Mayor Perez have a financial interest which prohibits him from participating in decisions involving the proposed Specific Plan? 2. Does the answer to the first question depend on whether a redevelopment project, covering the same area as the specific Plan, also is adopted?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Mayor Perez has a disqualifying financial interest in decisions regarding a proposed Downtown Specific Plan, given that his wife is employed by a business located in the Plan Area that could potentially be affected by future implementation through property demolition.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter (not an FPPC response). The document contains two numbered questions for FPPC advice. No conclusion/answer section present as this is a request document. The letter provides extensive factual background about the Specific Plan, the business location, and potential future impacts.

embedding.summary: City Attorney's request for formal written advice from the FPPC regarding whether Mayor Louis R. Perez has a prohibited financial interest in participating in decisions regarding a proposed Downtown Specific Plan for La Puente, where his wife is employed by a business in the plan area.
